muscleRoles.notes: Longus colli/longus capitis drive craniocervical flexion and the 'chin straight back' retraction — the target muscles being trained/activated.
Upper-cervical extensors directly stretched by the overpressure as the base of the skull nods and the back of the neck lengthens.
Posterior cervical extensor lengthened by retraction/upper-cervical flexion.
Posterior cervical extensor stretched as the head glides back and nods.
Upper-cervical attachment placed on stretch with retraction.
Posterior neck muscle lengthened as the cervical spine straightens and the chin tucks back.
Prime mover: extends the great toe at the first MTP (and IP) joint — the isolated hallux lift this drill trains.
Antagonist to hallux extension; lengthened/put on stretch as the big toe lifts.
Holds the lesser four toes pressed firmly into the floor so the hallux can be isolated.
Intrinsics help keep the lesser toes flexed/planted while the big toe extends independently.
Presses the 5th toe down, contributing to keeping the lesser toes anchored.

unmappedWanted: extensor-hallucis-brevis (additional prime hallux extensor, not in roster)
flexor-hallucis-brevis (short hallux flexor, would be lengthening antagonist, not in roster)
flexor-digitorum-brevis (intrinsic that anchors lesser toes, not in roster)
first-mtp-extension / hallux-extension movement (joint action producing the lift; no toe/MTP extension movement in roster)

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover for shoulder horizontal abduction, drawing the arms out to the sides against band resistance.
Retracts the scapula, squeezing the shoulder blades together as cued.
Scapular retraction; the target for 'squeeze the shoulder blades, not the upper traps'.
Assists horizontal abduction and provides external rotation of the humerus with palms-down grip.
Assists posterior deltoid in horizontal abduction and external rotation.
Depresses and stabilizes the scapula to keep the shoulder blades down, away from the upper traps.
Stabilizes the scapula against the rib cage during retraction.
Antagonist to horizontal abduction; stretched as the arms open out to the end range.
Antagonist stretched as the humerus moves into horizontal abduction.
Prime mover holding hip extension to maintain the bridge at the top throughout the marches.
Assist gluteus maximus in sustaining hip extension of the loaded/supporting leg.
Drives the march — hip flexion lifting each knee toward the chest.
Assists hip flexion of the marching leg.
Keeps the pelvis level when weight shifts to a single support leg during each march (prevents drop/rock).
Works with gluteus medius for frontal-plane pelvic stability during single-leg loading.
Deep anti-rotation core control keeping the pelvis from rocking as legs alternate.
Resists pelvic/trunk rotation — the anti-rotation demand of alternating marches.
Resists pelvic/trunk rotation to hold the pelvis level.
Controls lateral pelvic tilt, keeping the hips level ('glass of water on each hip').
Braces the trunk and helps maintain a level pelvis during the bridge hold.
Target of the maximal isometric squeeze; primary hip extensor and external rotator being reactivated after prolonged sitting.
Co-contracts during a maximal glute squeeze; upper fibers assist extension and provide pelvic/hip control.
Deep glute that co-activates with the maximal squeeze contributing to hip stabilization.
Assist hip extension as posterior-chain synergists during the maximal isometric contraction.
Prime mover extending the hip of the loaded (heel-on-floor) leg to lift the pelvis; the held-knee-to-chest lock biases work toward the glute rather than the hamstrings.
Posterior (extensor) fibers assist hip extension of the lifting leg.
Assist hip extension; deliberately de-emphasized here (hamstring cramp on the lifting leg is the cue to lower the hip).
Keeps the pelvis level and prevents drop/rotation during the single-leg lift.
Assists frontal/transverse-plane pelvic control on the stance leg.
Provides lumbopelvic bracing to keep the pelvis level and rotation-free.
Holds the posterior pelvic tilt, preventing lumbar hyperextension at the top.
Anti-rotation control so the bilateral pelvis stays square.
Anti-rotation control so the bilateral pelvis stays square.
Resists lateral pelvic drop and keeps the pelvis level on the single-leg base.
On the held leg, sustains hip flexion that locks the opposite hemipelvis and helps maintain the posterior tilt.
Stabilizes the flexed knee of the loaded leg as the hip extends.
Prime hip abductor driving the sideways step against band resistance; the 'stay low' cue targets its work
Deep hip abductor working with gluteus medius to move the leg out and stabilize the pelvis
Assists hip abduction with feet pointed forward and helps stabilize the pelvis
Upper fibers assist hip abduction/external rotation and control the trailing leg's return step
Isometrically holds the quarter-squat (mini-squat) position throughout the walk
Braces the core to keep the hips level and prevent trunk sway during lateral stepping
Primary extrinsic dynamic supporter of the medial longitudinal arch; assists the intrinsics in doming/shortening the foot and supinating.
Plantarflexes the first ray and locks the midfoot, supporting arch elevation during the short-foot action.
Co-activates to stabilize the hallux and support the medial arch, though the cue is to keep toes long and minimize its toe-curling contribution.
Invertor/dorsiflexor that helps control foot posture and support the medial arch while the toes stay long.
